solutions.solution: Definitely false. (See last sentence.)
Data inadequate. There is no mention of other countries.
Data inadequate. The first sentence says that the BIS scheme has certified products but no information is given about products that are not certified.

Probably true. Logically if 8 laboratories can issue 42000 test reports, then, on an average, one laboratory can issue about 5250 test reports [42000 ÷ 8 = 5250]. But as modernisation of laboratories has not been clearly stated that all laboratories have equal capacity.
Definitely false. Read the third sentence. It says that these laboratories are being "constantly expanded ... and modernised."
Probably true. The first sentence says that the BIS does provide testing. So, it is likely that the body is autonomous.
Definitely true. The first sentence says that there would be "constantly equipped and modernise laboratories?"
Definitely true. The first sentence mentions three avowed goals of elementary education: learner achievement apart from access to elementary education. Note that apart from means in addition to.

Definitely true. Elementary education is, of course, a duty of the government. But the second sentence mentions "investment in elementary education". This means that elementary education is a type of investment too.
Probably true. The failure implies that the planners probably did not pay due attention to this aspect.
Probably false. The passage neither claims nor contradicts that NCERT has evolved such body. But our commonsense tells us that this is true, NCERT being false.
Data inadequate. There has not been any mention of any improvement after the investment.
Definitely true. The first sentence says that India's health is poor but health status has improved. This means that the given inference is true.

Probably true. Infant survival conditions have improved but read the first sentence that says that health status has improved, but is still poor. Most likely, the improvement has been from 'very bad' to 'so so'.

Definitely false. See the last sentence ("inter-regional disparities") reports of UNDP are the only authentic reports, not ours nor denied that reports of UNDP are the only authentic reports, our commonsense tells this too.
Data inadequate. No doubt India has improved vastly from 1951 to 1992. Yet we must also know the performance of other countries to reach any definite conclusion.
Definitely false. The first sentence clearly says that liberalisation does not imply "no regulation at all".
Probably true. The whole passage is on this very theme; that regulatory should not be excessive but there must be some regulation and that must be socially sensitive.
Definitely true. Otherwise the financial system will display "its own excesses".

Data inadequate. The passage mentions the chances of excessive speculation in the capital markets (i.e., stock markets) in the age of liberalisation but no information is presented about what used to happen in the earlier time.
Probably true. Although there is no definite information yet the meritorious news that forth indicate that the author favours economic very well.

The last five says that if excessive emphasis is laid on economic growth, it will postpone social welfare and leave "the cold substantial section of the society." This and the overall tone of the passage indicate that a major plank of our society does need social welfare programmes. But still, the inference is 'probably true' and not 'definitely true'. The reason is the word desperate. It is not explicitly stated that people 'desperately' need social welfare programmes.

At one time the FM said that welfare programmes would start once the fiscal imbalances were controlled. He now says that these programmes would start only after growth gathers momentum. It is clear that he is not consistent in his thought.

The author complains that the FM has not fulfilled his promise of starting welfare programmes after fiscal imbalances were controlled. His complaint obviously implies that fiscal imbalances have been controlled and yet welfare programmes have not been started. Also, the FM now says that welfare programmes would start after growth gains momentum. But economic growth cannot be possible in a state of fiscal imbalance. So, fiscal imbalances must have cleared up by now.
Obviously.

It is implied that the FM promises to start welfare programmes once growth gathers momentum. This, he says, may 'postpone indefinitely' the promise of reform with a human face. The use of the word 'indefinitely' is quite quick. However, it is not explicitly stated that this process is time-taking. Hence, the inference is probably false but not definitely false.

The first line talks about a country having a dominant public sector while, in the same breath, another line mentions that private sector in India should, therefore, change. It is obvious that the passage is talking about India. Hence the inference is definitely true.
Definitely false. The last line implies just the opposite: that private sector need to ignore technological changes.
